comment_text.sn24.7:1.3: Makkhali Gosāla’s doctrine of no-cause (ahetukavāda), see SN 22.60:2.2, DN 2:20.2.

root_text.sn24.7:1.2: “Kismiṁ nu kho, bhikkhave, sati, kiṁ upādāya, kiṁ abhinivissa evaṁ diṭṭhi uppajjati:
root_text.sn24.7:1.5: Natthi hetu, natthi paccayo sattānaṁ visuddhiyā.
root_text.sn24.7:1.7: Natthi balaṁ natthi vīriyaṁ natthi purisathāmo natthi purisaparakkamo.
root_text.sn24.7:1.8: Sabbe sattā sabbe pāṇā sabbe bhūtā sabbe jīvā avasā abalā avīriyā niyatisaṅgatibhāvapariṇatā chasvevābhijātīsu sukhadukkhaṁ paṭisaṁvedentī’”ti?
root_text.sn24.7:2.1: “Rūpe kho, bhikkhave, sati, rūpaṁ upādāya, rūpaṁ abhinivissa evaṁ diṭṭhi uppajjati:
root_text.sn24.7:2.7: viññāṇe sati, viññāṇaṁ upādāya, viññāṇaṁ abhinivissa evaṁ diṭṭhi uppajjati:
root_text.sn24.7:3.4: vipariṇāmadhammaṁ, api nu taṁ anupādāya evaṁ diṭṭhi uppajjeyya:
root_text.sn24.7:3.13: api nu taṁ anupādāya evaṁ diṭṭhi uppajjeyya:
root_text.sn24.7:3.17: “Yampidaṁ diṭṭhaṁ sutaṁ mutaṁ viññātaṁ pattaṁ pariyesitaṁ anuvicaritaṁ manasā tampi niccaṁ vā aniccaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn24.7:3.19: api nu taṁ anupādāya evaṁ diṭṭhi uppajjeyya:
root_text.sn24.7:4.1: “Yato kho, bhikkhave, ariyasāvakassa imesu ca ṭhānesu kaṅkhā pahīnā hoti, dukkhepissa kaṅkhā pahīnā hoti …pe… dukkhanirodhagāminiyā paṭipadāyapissa kaṅkhā pahīnā hoti—
root_text.sn24.7:4.2: ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako sotāpanno avinipātadhammo niyato sambodhiparāyano”ti.

translation_text.sn24.7:1.2: “Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what and insisting on what, does the view arise:
translation_text.sn24.7:1.3: ‘There is no cause or reason for the corruption of sentient beings.
translation_text.sn24.7:1.4: Sentient beings are corrupted without cause or reason.
translation_text.sn24.7:1.5: There’s no cause or reason for the purification of sentient beings.
translation_text.sn24.7:1.6: Sentient beings are purified without cause or reason.
translation_text.sn24.7:1.7: There is no power, no energy, no human strength or vigor.
translation_text.sn24.7:1.8: All sentient beings, all living creatures, all beings, all souls lack control, power, and energy. Molded by destiny, circumstance, and nature, they experience pleasure and pain in the six classes of rebirth’?”
translation_text.sn24.7:1.9: “Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …”
translation_text.sn24.7:2.1: “When form exists, because of grasping form and insisting on form, the view arises:
translation_text.sn24.7:2.3: they experience pleasure and pain in the six classes of rebirth.’
translation_text.sn24.7:2.7: consciousness exists, because of grasping consciousness and insisting on consciousness, the view arises:
translation_text.sn24.7:2.9: they experience pleasure and pain in the six classes of rebirth.’
translation_text.sn24.7:3.17: “That which is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, and explored by the mind: is that permanent or impermanent?”
translation_text.sn24.7:3.19: “But by not grasping what’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, would such a view arise?”
translation_text.sn24.7:4.1: “When a noble disciple has given up doubt regarding these six grounds, and has given up doubt in suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation,
translation_text.sn24.7:4.2: they’re called a noble disciple who is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, destined for awakening.”
